comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight as I type this, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. This week's classic of Western occultism is The Tarot by Mouni Sadhu. Sadhu's actual name was Mieczyslaw Demetriusz Sudowski, he studied occultism in Poland before the Second World War and ended up as a naturalized Australian citizen after the war, and he's one of the very few sources in English on the French system of occultism that spread east to Poland and Russia in the late 19th century. It's a mixed bag in some ways -- the author's overheated disquisition on the evils of masturbation is one of the funniest works of unintentional comedy in print -- but The Tarot is an extremely thorough guide to Hermetic occultism, and builds on the foundations of Eliphas Lévi's Doctrine and Ritual of High Magic in a great many useful ways. 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed -- and yes, this means you. See you next week!***

comments.title: Tarot
comments.content:
Funny that you post a Tarot book, since I was debating whether or not to ask you a Tarot-related question.

I’m just beginning to explore the Tarot, and I wonder if you have any specific books or resources you, with your unique perspective, would recommend.

Also, I’m wondering whether you think Tarot counts as magic under your specific definition about altering the state of consciousness of the practitioner. I am more interested in it as a form of meditation/introspection than divination.

replies.title: Re: Tarot
replies.content: The Tarot is the Swiss army knife of the Western occultist -- 78 blades, each with a different function! ;-) Seriously, it's one of the core tools of the tradition, and divination is only one of its many uses. As a source of themes for meditation, and of imaginative doorways for the exercise that occultists call Pathworking and Jung called "active imagination," it's unrivaled. Two books by Gareth Knight -- The Magical World of the Tarot and The Treasure House of Images aka Tarot and Magic -- will give you plenty of material to work with.

replies.title: Re: Tarot
replies.content:

comments.content:
Hi JMG,

1. Why do you think that Tarot, astrology, yoga, and other ‘alternative’ spiritual practices have become so strongly associated with women in recent decades? Lots of popular books, articles and other materials on these topics are created on the assumption that the audience will be overwhelmingly female. Correct me if I’m wrong, but this seems like a historical novelty.

2. One the common characterizations of Jews in conspiracy and anti-Semitic literature is that they’re secretive, closed off, and are plotting to take over the world. Do you think this perception might be a Christian projection of the Shadow? It’s an interesting bit of historical trivia that it was the Christians of the first four centuries that met all of those criteria, rejecting mainstream Classical society and claiming a monopoly on religious truth (later enforced by violence when what became the institutional Church gained political power). In effect, Christians did have a plan to take over the world and implemented that plan successfully. Could there be a collective guilt in the Christian psyche that was externalized onto the Jews?

3. Is there any occult significance to the US flag having 50 pentagrams on it, and would it make any difference to add or subtract any of them? I know that the US has had different numbers of 'stars' in different configurations over the course of its history, but the current 50 have been around for long enough that we can probably assume it's the definitive form.

4. What are your thoughts on the concept of 'mental illness' as an organic disease as currently promoted by the medical and psychiatric establishment? Obviously, the profit motive and corruption of the pharmaceutical industry can be pointed out here, and on the other end of the spectrum, the psychiatrist Thomas Szasz famously argued that it's a category error to apply a model of biological illness to the mind, which is not a biological process. Do you see any middle ground between these two extremes?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) It's not really a novelty, at least not in the English-speaking world. Back in the day, religion was one of the very few fields of activity where women were allowed an active role in public life; by the 17th century, you had women taking the lead in alternative religious circles, and major 19th-century alternative religious movements such as Theosophy and Christian Science were run by women and largely staffed by them as well. Cultural habits all through the English-speaking world continue to remind women that alternative spirituality is one field where there's no glass ceiling.

2) That's doubtless part of it, yes. Another part is a kind of inferiority complex that's hardwired into most versions of Christian theology -- after all, if you accept the Old Testament, the Jews were God's original chosen people, and the various Christian countries are Johnny-come-latelies.

3) The current 50 have only been around since 1959, when Alaska and Hawai'i became states. It's entirely possible that there will be 52 in the not too distant future, if the various movements to split California into multiple states bear fruit. Thus I don't think it matters how many stars there are.

4) Sure. Szasz's extreme is indefensible -- I can guarantee you that if the wholly biological event of a stroke or a concussion affects your brain, it will affect your mind as well -- and so is that of the medical establishment, which simply wants to sell as many pills as possible. In the middle is the recognition that some mental conditions do seem to have a biological origin, some do not, and still others seem to combine biological and nonbiological origins. A sane science of psychiatry would work on differentiating among these.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Do you think that a sane psychiatry would focus more on etheric levels in order to effect changes that simultaneously trickle down to the brain and other aspects of the mind both physical and non-physical? Would this potentially be a way out of the dilemma? I mean, I'm sure it's possible that science could come understand the brain better if it really focused its efforts on doing so - if it wasn't so corrupted by Mammon. But the brain seems like such a complex, delicate thing, I find it hard to see how blasting it with chemicals could ever be anything other than a very rough technic with inevitable, serious downsides.

replies.title: (no subject)

There are forms of mental illness that really do seem to be physically based, and since the planes are discrete and not continuous, a physical ailment needs a physical treatment. Those modes of illness that are primarily etheric, astral, or mental, however, need to be treated accordingly -- and it would probably be wisest, in dealing with any such illness that isn't obviously purely physical, to start on the mental plane anyway and work step by step downward from there.

replies.content:
I'm a former Satanist so I may be able to shed some light on this.

The answer lies in the "pop" prefix. In America, there is a very limited view of the occult and magic. There's Christian magic, which sounds oxymoronic to those who are starting out, and despite there being a abundance of it in the West, always feels out of place due to mainstream Christianity's hatred of the occult. New Age and Wicca that feel as if they are for women. And there's the Dark Side. (I know I'm oversimplifying but I'm in a hurry and writing this on a small tablet.) The last is so popular because it is so frequently depicted in Media and those who turn to the occult are usually dissatisfied with Christianity to begin with.

Early on I became unhappy with the lack of depth in Satanism after I started reading widely but found no alternatives. The Christian influence was extremely pervasive and I felt a strong aversion to it. I wasn't drawn to New Age or Wicca. I heard from someone that there were people worshipping the Greek and Heathen gods which I reacted to in the same way as when I learned of Otherkin. Druidry didn't get so much as a chuckle when I heard there were modern day ones. I assumed Druids were Wiccans too embarrassed to call themselves Wiccan. In the case of Hellenism and Heathenism my dismissiveness was that it was so far from the image I had of serious occultism, the image that came almost entirely from the media, and the then unknown influence of Progress, the notion we had moved past such things.

TL;DR: The image of the occultist is of a dark shadowy figure thanks to the Media and Western history and most are happy to play their assigned parts.

This raises the controversial issue of intellectual copyright, but as a person who is actually involved in the business of writing, you may have a better grasp of this than I do.

replies.title: (no subject)

From my perspective, there's no difference at all, because either act deprives me of part of my livelihood. I don't own the copies of my books on sale at bookstores -- the bookstores own them -- but their theft makes it harder for me to support myself by my writing. The same is true of e-theft.

As a writer, I get between $1 and $2 for every copy of one of my books that somebody buys. That's what pays my bills and makes it possible for me to devote all my work hours to research, study, and writing. Those who steal my work, electronically or otherwise, are stealing my income just as much as though they mugged me and ran off with my wallet. If someone is going to do that, I feel it's perfectly reasonable for me to return the favor, and see to it that they get no benefit from their theft.

If I had to flip burgers for a living, I couldn't spare the time for that, or for the translations I do (very few of which bring in much income), or some of the other activities I do to pay forward the help I received when I was first getting into magic.

I have a difficult question. Jason Miller said: "Don't be afraid to be firm with the gods." In my life very recently, my woman who is a foreigner has been prevented twice now from coming to my country, first by the action of robbers and secondly by the death of her aunt, both very sudden and sad occurrences. Of course, I am certain that this is not mere "bad luck and circumstance"; I have thrown all of my admittedly very novice sorcerous skills into this very endeavor; I thank God and the gods that she and I have met, but I am becoming very angry and upset that our meeting has been stymied by seemingly "random" occurrence, especially as I carry out a daily theurgic worship of the day's ruling Deity, to include a candle, an offering of water and incense, and a recitation of the Deity's Orphic hymn. replies.title: Re: Working alone
replies.content:
1) This is completely normal for a beginner. If you'd never tried to play a musical instrument before, and you decided to start learning, you'd be about at the same stage in terms of your playing after three months. Magic isn't easy; it takes the same kind of patient, relentless practice that music does.

2) I don't recommend that beginners join groups. There are some good magical orders out there, but most of them are very slow to accept new members, and there are a far, far greater number of groups that are useless or actively harmful. It's usually far better to study and practice on your own for at least a few years, so that you develop some skill and also some knowledge, and have some basis to assess any group you encounter thereafter.

3) There's no one path. You choose a system for your initial work and follow it -- and that's what you've done.

replies.title: Re: Emotional Awareness
replies.content: Magic as such doesn't address this to any great degree, but there are certainly resources. Do-In was very popular for a while in the 1970s and seems to have fallen into almost complete oblivion since then. comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear Archdruid,

I have two questions about tarot that are related to each other:
1 - There are many legends and speculations about the historical origins of tarot in occultist circles. What do you think is the most plausible origin story?
2 - Was tarot invented by occultists in the first place or taken up by them later?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
One of the great weaknesses of modern occultism is that it so often fails to pay attention to what's actually known about history. Italian art historians found out many years ago that the earliest version of the tarot was invented between 1418 and 1425 in Milan by Duke Filippo Maria Visconti and/or his secretary Marziano da Tortona. The trumps as we now know them are one of dozens of competing versions known in late Renaissance Italy, which were inspired by the original deck. The tarot was originally a deck for playing card games -- tarocchi, the game played with them, is still popular in some parts of Europe.

The first known use of tarot cards for divination was around 1750, at which time just about every kind of cards were used for divination; the rise of the tarot to its current position as the most widely used divinatory deck didn't begin until J.-B. Aliette ("Eteilla") began using them as a divinatory deck in Paris in the 1780s. Once that happened, though, occultists went to work on the deck, expanded and adapted its symbolism, rearranged the trumps as needed (in most modern decks, Justice and Strength have been switched from their original positions), and turned it into the superbly functional occult tool it has become.

The same thing could be done just as well with other decks; there's some very creative work being done these days with the Lenormand deck, for example. The more the merrier!

replies.title: Re: Sola Busca tarot
replies.content: The Rider-Waite tarot, the Knapp-Hall tarot, the Golden Dawn tarot, the Oswald Wirth tarot, the Thoth tarot, and the Esoteric Masonic tarot are good examples of decks of this vintage designed by occultists, and loaded with occult symbolism.

replies.title: Re: Sola Busca tarot
replies.content:
I'm not JMG, but I am a historian with a background in Tarot and playing card history, so I hope neither of you mind if I chime in.

In my opinion, the Sola Busca Tarot was developed as a teaching tool, as many early Tarot decks were. The Sola Busca is based on the history and mythical history of ancient Rome (ie what people at the time believed about ancient Rome, some of which was mythology rather than actual history), and the figures depicted in the cards represent both good examples of people to emulate and bad examples of people to avoid emulating. This was bog standard in Renaissance Italian Tarot decks, which were widely used (in the form of teaching games) to train the children of the upper classes in proper behavior, good citizenship, etc. We've got loads of documentation on the didactic uses of early Tarot.

On the other hand, Peter Mark Adams' theories, given in his book The Game of Saturn, have almost no credible basis in actual fact. Further, some of his claims (the Ammon material, in particular) go in direct opposition to the actual iconography and symbolism of the deck, and to documented historical fact. Frankly, if Mr. Adams said that the sky is blue, I'd look out the window to fact-check him.

That said, given that a lot of black magicians have apparently fallen for Mr. Adams' theories, they may currently be using the Sola Busca for black magic, which could produce a tainted egregore. My own copy of the deck seems to be quite clean and unimpaired, but YMMV. Then again, I don't use it for divination, but for study of symbolic images, which is a different ball game.

comments.title: A lesson in muscle testing
comments.content:
On the other blog Scotlyn asked, “So I am very interested, but completely untutored, in this idea of using muscle testing to communicate with a body, whether it is a patient’s body (which I will seek further learning sources to improve upon) or my own body. In relation to the second, though, I wonder would it be a big ask for you to briefly illustrate with a step by step example of you DOING this? Eg. Question, technique, reaction, answer. The HOW of it. Also, if the answer sometimes might be “I don’t want to tell you” how do you distinguish that from a “yes” or a “no”?

It turns out there are a lot more details to describe here than I had expected. So let’s call this a first lesson, describing the most basic form of muscle testing. There’s certainly plenty here to practice and mull over for a week. Next week I can describe testing physical imbalances on oneself, without a partner. Then the week after that I can describe testing yes/no questions (I assume that is what the Japanese acupuncturists are doing with the O-ring technique, though they may be directly testing the flow of chi). The danger of trying to steal the body’s wisdom and needing to be able to distinguish “I don’t want to tell you” responses only comes in when you start muscle testing questions. So today I only went into the physical how of it:

By far the easiest form of muscle testing is doing it on another person to test the physical imbalances currently in their body rather than trying to test questions being held in your mind. As an acupuncturist, you already know how you get a sense of what imbalances a client is experiencing and then test out your hypotheses by asking them questions, comparing pulses, and noting their body’s response to your needle placement. This form of muscle testing will add another way you can test out your hypotheses/hunches. If the client is lying supine on the table, the easiest way to test is by having them hold an arm out to the side in crucifixion position; you then press gently floorward on their forearm while they resist to test the neutral strength of their pecs. Keeping their neutral strength in mind, you touch a point on their body which you believe is somehow compromised and press down on their forearm again. If they test weaker, your hunch was accurate. If they test as strongly as before, move on to test your next hunch.

For acupuncture this might follow the taking of pulses in order to verify which meridian showed up weak, problematic, or distrupted. If touching the contact point for the meridian tests weak, you can then have the client touch that contact point to maintain that weakening effect, while you either touch points along that meridian or the contact point of another meridian to discover what point will re-strengthen their body when you again press down on their forearm. Thus you can test out some pretty complex interactions among the meridians this way before having to commit to needle placement. This testing is particularly useful when multiple meridians have gone haywire, as you can test through the sequence of the meridians to discover which one has thrown the others off, whether by being too weak or too strong. Obviously, you would want to get some practice with friends or coworkers before trying this with clients.

Let’s move on to testing current physical imbalances outside a clinical setting. Either you or your friend can be the one looking to decode an imbalance, or you can alternate back and forth. Whatever the case, at any given time one of you will be the subject and the other will be the aide. The subject usually sits or stands with an arm outstretched in crucifixion position, though any muscle can be used effectively for testing. Because the lateral deltoid is relatively weak, the aide can press floorward at the elbow to prevent muscle fatigue, and the subject can change the arm being tested as needed. Either party can touch points on the subject’s body where an imbalance is suspected.

Since pain often gives us only a vague idea of the source of disorder, testing larger areas than a fingertip can be very useful. Subject or aide can place an entire hand flat on a suspected region of the subject’s body and move their hand around following their hunches until the subject’s arm tests weak, indicating that a point that needs work is somewhere under where the hand is making contact. Without removing the hand completely, lift it until only the four finger tips are touching in a line and test the subject’s arm’s strength. Slide the finger tips back over where the flat hand had been touching, while repeatedly testing until the subject’s arm weakens again. Now lift all but one finger and test each of the fingers separately until the subject’s arm weakens again. That is the point of imbalance that the body was signaling it would like to have worked on somehow. If the point is in no way tender, you can test a little halo of points around it to find the exact point that tests the weakest, and hopefully at least a little tender. It sounds awfully complex and slow, but it is quite a bit faster than randomly touching tiny points on the body until you stumble on a winner. Knowing your body and its habitual imbalances and compensations helps considerably.

There are all kinds of ways to beneficially work on the points the body reveals, but the most common by far is simply to press on them until their tenderness goes away. Yes, that’s called acupressure. When does your body feel you have pressed a point long enough? When you can touch it and have your arm test strong again. Will there be points where no amount of acupressure can balance them? Of course. Those points may be revealing a more complex underlying disorder, or they may just want different attention. Rubbing the spot or tapping it with your fingers may be all that’s needed. On the other hand, if you have found a subluxation in your spine, your body probably wants it adjusted back into alignment. If you have found an abscess, your body certainly wants it treated in some way other than by pressing on it! There is a lot of nurturance and rebalancing you can do on your own, but by no means can you fix every problem. Some a professional can help with, and some we just have to live with.

Anything that presses on or distorts the body can be muscle tested for its benefits/drawbacks. You may really love that pair of shoes even though they’re a size too small, but your body may hate them. Test your strength barefoot and then test with the shoes on — if it drops considerably, you might not want to wear them. Some things are only subtly imbalancing, so you might have to walk for a whole day in those shoes before your body would test noticeably weaker than walking a whole day in comfortable shoes. If you are a chronic pronator with a pair of orthotics that just don’t seem right, test with and without them — if you test weaker with them in your shoes, they are causing more imbalance than they are correcting for.

OK, I did get a problematic pair of orthotics from a podiatrist who would not believe that my interphalangeal joint was injured, not my metatrasophalangeal joint (IP joint injuries are a blind spot for podiatrists). By having my boyfriend test my strength while I stood on the orthotics and adjusted little cardboard wedges under them, we determined where the orthotics needed felt pads glued to them. It was so successful that we went on to test and alter the orthotics while I stood in relevé and then just on the injured foot in relevé, mimicking the push-off phase of a regular gait. Lo and behold, the pain and swelling in my big toe, which I had lived with for four excruciating years, disappeared in under a week. The body knows what it needs! Given the test, treat/alter, test again nature of partnered muscle testing, it is great if the aide/boyfriend is doing something that can be easily interrupted and gone back to. If either the subject or the aide gets annoyed with the other, it can turn into a miserable process. Don’t do that to each other. Try again later or find another muscle testing partner.

— Christophe

For example, during the Cold War, both the US and USSR governments employed dozens of psychic spies.

Additionally, more than a hundred years ago, William Walker Atkinson (even though himself was deeply involved in occultism) wrote these lines in a book:
"This is one of the most powerful forms of Influencing
En Masse, and is used with great effect by many of the 'strong

replies.title: Re: Conspiracy theories in occultism
replies.content:
Okay, let's cover some basics first. Do conspiracies exist? Of course. Are you going to find out the truth about them by hanging breathlessly on anonymous claims splashed all over the internet? Um, no. Anonymous leaks to the gullible are among the standard modalities by which actual conspiracies spread their disinformation. Look into the history of the UFO phenomenon, for example, and notice how often claims that support this or that story of alien contact are being spread by someone with connections to US Air Force intelligence. That's not accidental; the UFO brouhaha has been the most important form of camouflage the Air Force uses for secret weapons testing since 1947, when it was originally used to distract attention from high altitude balloon tests. The U-2, the SR-47, the first two generations or so of spy satellites, stealth technology (remember all those UFOs that looked like black triangles?) -- those and much more sheltered behind a mythology of alien contact that was systematically spread by the Air Force as disinformation.

The whole Qanon business is more of the same. Who exactly is behind it, and what their specific goals are, is an interesting question I don't expect ever to be able to answer. The one thing you can be sure of, in dealing with any of these supposed "leaks," is that an assortment of government agencies are aware of every word that everyone posts regarding them -- nothing anywhere on the internet, remember, is actually private -- and records are being kept. That means among other things that none of the supposed leakers are actually leaking anything that could land them in legal trouble. Just as in the UFO business, when somebody official claims to be giving you secret information that nobody in power wants you to have, assume that it's disinformation that someone in power wants you to believe and you won't go wrong.

Finally, the notion that we're all helpless pawns of some omnipotent cabal -- or of anything else -- is about as far as you can get from genuine occultism. At the heart of the occult path is the recognition that, as Paul Foster Case put it, "All the power that ever was or will be is here now." The power you have over your life is limited only by your willingness to embrace it and accept the consequences. Fairly often, the reason people flee into conspiracy theories and the like is that it allows them to blame someone else for their own unwillingness to accept their own power and their own potential for greatness...with all the risks and challenges that acceptance brings with it.

replies.title: Re: Conspiracy theories in occultism
replies.content: I don't really accept any of the conspiracy theories I have encountered and most of them seem moderately absurd; I do respect my friend enough to at least listen to what he has to say on occult topics, although sometimes his insistence on the truth of these things makes me uncomfortable. Not having friends or contacts in the occult world, it's sometimes difficult (for me) to discern what is garbage or what has some legitimacy. Sometimes it seems like a chunk of the world has developed a case of schizophrenia; I have run into conspiracy theorists outside the occult world enough to wonder if I am somehow attracting these types of people into my sphere, so to speak. Thank you for your answer.

comments.title: Occult Philosophy

replies.title: Re: Occult Philosophy
replies.content: It depends on the occultist. Blavatsky was trying to define occultism as a third force, distinct from the standpoints of orthodox religion and materialist science and standing aside from their endless quarrels. Some occultists still hold to her theory, but many others don't. The Druid Revival tradition embraced a version of evolution not too far from Darwin's long before Darwin published The Origin of Species -- you'll find it in detail in the writings of Iolo Morganwg, who died in 1826. replies.content:

One of my favourite books is Secret Wisdom : The Occult Universe Revealed by David Conway. He sums it up this way and it's an answer I've always been content with, he states ...

"To avoid misunderstanding, let me make plain at once that no self-respecting occultist would nowadays deny that man's physical body evolved, in it's final stages, from ape to hominid and from hominid to man. The external view of evolution sees in this only the cumulative effect of a long series of gradual, perhaps accidental, mutations which results in a qualitative, as well as quantitative, change in the whole, the latter thereby becoming greater than the sum of its constituent parts. Occultists, on the other hand, would insist that evolution necessarily means that such qualitative innovations are possible only because they are already implicit in the simple unity from which they have slowly evolved : thanks to evolution, the universe does not BECOME greater than the sum of its elements, it merely expresses through them it's own antecedent greatness. The more complex and numerous the material elements available to it at any time, the more it can reveal of itself, just as a thinker can better express his thoughts, the more comprehensive his vocabulary happens to be."

Cheers, Steve

comments.title: Geomancy
comments.content:
Salutations JMG, long time lurker, first time poster here.
I've been excitedly delving into Geomancy with the help of your excellent book on the subject, but I've been having some significant difficulty. I'll try to pare my befuddlement down to a few questions:

1) How important is the Reconciler to the shield chart? Frequently I've done many readings that seem to say "Some difficulty in the past, but through hard work you can achieve your goal" but when the reconciler is brought into the chart the reading suddenly shifts to "doom and gloom, you will fail" with a figure like Carcer or Cauda Draconis. The reconciler is "the result of the result" right? When is it appropriate to bring the Reconciler into the question, beyond when the chart seems unclear? For me it keeps throwing both charts into chaos by reversing the judgement rather than clarifying it.

2) In a house chart, is the meaning of the figures or the connections between the houses more important? Are the mechanics of the house chart tightly bound to the question asked, or their own connections? For example, I asked "Will I catch the flu" and got RW: Carcer, Judge: Amissio, LW: Fortuna Major. Sounds pretty good right? Through isolation (Carcer) I will not possess (Amissio) the flu, and although the isolation may be long and tough the future looks bright (Fortuna Major). But when I put the figures into a house chart, Carcer performs a translation between the 1st House and the 6th House, which contains Amissio again. Initially I interpreted this as the quarantine being the third factor that prevents me from catching the flu (Amissio). But on further rereading (and rereading, and highlighting, and rereading again) I'm not so sure.

Does the perfection of a chart like this always mean "yes" even when you're hoping for a no? Which is more important, the mechanics of the house chart tying directly into the question, or the meaning of the figures to the diviner? I encountered a similar issue when asking "Will my friend catch the flu" and received a direct Occupation of the 6th house with Fortuna Major. Is the fortunate figure more important here, or is the mechanics of the house chart (perfection always equals "yes") the important thing?
I guess I'm asking if the house chart answers "Yes/No" or "Fortunate/Unfortunate for the Querent."

3) If the figure of the Quesited passes to a different house (say, Albus in the 7th passing to the 6th for a question about love), can a third figure perform a Perfection by Translation between the first house and the passed figure? Again, is the figure more important or are the house chart mechanics the guiding force?

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content:
1) I rarely use it. The witnesses and judge are usually enough to give a clear answer.

2) Okay, first of all, don't take the figures from a shield chart and plop them into a house chart. Choose one or the other and stick with that, or you'll end up always being confused. If you set out to do a shield chart, leave it at that -- and in that case, the answer is no, you will not catch the flu; staying at home (Carcer) will cause you not to catch it (Amissio) and everything will be fine (Fortuna Major).

If you set out to do a house chart, the presence or absence of a mode of perfection gives you a yes or no. The character of the figure or figures in the houses of querent and quesited tell you whether it will be fortunate or unfortunate.

3) No. A translation is only valid if it's between the houses of the querent and the quesited; you can't add up a mutation and a translation to make a chart perfect! Again, if you have one of the standard modes of perfection, the answer is yes; if not, it's no; and the characteristics of the figure tell you how that "yes" or "no" will affect you and the quesited.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content:
2) Oh really? Wow, yeah I've been doing a shield chart and then a house chart. I guess that would screw things up, no wonder I've been having so much trouble. Is there a specific reason that you can do only one or the other?

Is there any benefit to casting a Shield chart instead of a House chart then? It seems the House chart gives a lot more detail into the "how" of the matter, but maybe there's different types of questions each chart is better suited to?

4) How closely do the Witnesses and Judge adhere to the past-present/result-future model? In chapter five you wrote "The figure that appears as Left Witness gives information about the quesited, but this information focuses on what the quesited means to the querent." For a question where you hope the answer is "No" how does a favourable figure interpret? Does it adhere strongly to the future-tense ("You escape breaking your leg, and are overjoyed") or does it only display how you feel about your desired outcome ("You would be very happy if you didn't break your leg, it's up to the Judge to decide how things play out")?

5) One thing that seemed intuitive to me was to follow the Way of the Points in the Shield chart and note which of the Four Triplicities it ended up in, so for a question about love if the Way of the Points ends up as Populous in the fourth Triplicity I might guess that I may meet a future love interest through a group of mutual friends, especially if Populous shows up as the left-most figure in the Fourth triplicity. Am I barking up the wrong tree again? Are the Triplicities and Way of the Points separate systems that do not interact, or can something be read from where the Way of the Points ends up within the Shield chart?

6) On page 123, discussing example 6-8 you write "...while that of the quesited is Via, which is an unfavorable figure in many contexts." Why is this so? I can understand that Via acts almost like a mirror, inverting many figures and changing bad into good and good into bad, but isn't "change" and "long journey" a neutral and slightly optimistic concept? Is there a mechanical reason, or is this a subject for further meditation?

To that end, how much of Geomancy differs between Geomancers and is thus a subject for meditation?

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content:
2) It depends on what you want to know. I find shield charts really useful for "what's the current situation and how did it get this way" questions -- combine the Way of Points with the four triplicities and you can unpack a lot of information from a reading. I find house charts really useful for "is this a workable option" questions.

4) I've never noticed geomancy caring much about how I felt about anything! As for the specific interpretation of a given Left Witness, daily practice and study will teach you the intuitive skills you need to tell which is which.

5) As noted above, I combine them all the time, and get good results.

6) It's very much a modern conceit that change always tends to be for the better and journeys always end in better places. The middle ages saw things very differently, and that's where the traditional lore comes from. My advice is to compare your readings to the events that follow them, and see how Via works out for you. As for your final question, that's also a good theme for meditation!

Put up with the discomfort, no matter how miserable it gets; in time the tensions and emotions will drain off, and you'll be much healthier and be able to go into deep relaxation any time you get into that posture. \

replies.content:
The "unpublished letter from Bowen" that is shown as evidence(!) for this hypothesis contains a lot of factual errors. Even if it did not contain any factual error, still a personal letter like this would not prove anything. Let's say I write a letter to someone which contains a claim like "I'm the actual author of Da Vinci Code, not Dan Brown." Would this prove anything?

Besides, the academic research of Philip Deslippe clearly shows that William Walker Atkinson is the sole author of The Kybalion, The Secret Doctrine of the Rosicrucians and the "Yogi Ramacharaka" books.

replies.title: Re: Dr Traverner and New Thought
replies.content:
There are some disquieting elements in that blog post:

1 - The author equates New Thought movement with New Age and popular self-help stuff like "The Secret" type of books, which is far from truth.
2 - The author treats New thought movement as something completely separate from genuine occultism. He should be reminded about the history of his own lineage - Dion Fortune herself had a background in Christian Science which is a branch of New Thought.
3 - The author treats Atkinson as an average New Thought writer. However, Atkinson was an initiate of Hermeticism and Theosophy - just like Theodore Moriarty was.
4 - The claims about the authorship of those books (The Kybalion and the other ones mentioned in the blog) require hard evidence, not some subjective statements written in a piece of private letter.

replies.title: Re: Dr Traverner and New Thought
replies.content:
On North's own showing, Moriarty remained in South Africa until about 1910, so any works preserved by his students came into circulation after that date. His own books of Masonic practice appeared a little before that, in 1908 and 1909.

On the other hand, Ramacharaka's "Fourteen Lessons in Occult Philosophy" was published as a correspondence course in 1903, and as a book in 1904. His "Advanced Course in Yogi Philosophy and Oriental occultism" appeared first as a book in 1905. The two volumes formed Ramacharaka's "Yogi Philosophy," vols. I and II. (Volumes III, IV and V in that series appeared in 1906, 1907 and 1908, but are not relevant to North's claims.) "The Kybalion" first appeared in 1908. These are years when Moriarty was still in South Africa and most heavily involved in writing and publishing his Masonic books.

"Yogi Ramacharaka" first appears as the author of any Yogi Publication Society volumes during the years when Atkinson himself was hiding out for a few years in Los Angeles (instead of living at home in Chicago), while one of his business associates in Chicago was having trouble with the law. From style and from content, it seems highy likely that "Ramacharaka" began his literary life as a practical means by which Atkinson could hide from the law; but it proved a marketable pseudonym, and Atkinsonb continued to use it as late as 1912.

Philip Deslippe has pretty convincingly shown that "The Kybalion" is also Atkinson's work, who thus is all three of the "Three Initiates" who wrote it. (Does the phrase "three initiates" include the authors "Akinson" and "Ramacharaka" alongside of the anonymous author of the Kybalion? Three in one, like the oil?)

"The Secret Doctrine of the Rosicrucians" is a different matter. It appeared in 1918, after Moriarty had returned to the UK from South Africa.

I should note that the official semi-annual Catalog of Copyright Entries includes only those books for which the author or published completed the entire process of obtaining a copyright, right down to the deposit of two copies in the Library of Congress. It does not include valid copyrights for which copies were never sent to the Library of Congress for deposit. Thus one needs also to check for copyright renewals a few decades later if one wants to get the full picture. As it happened, "The Secret Doctrine of the Rosicrucians" does not appear in this Catalogue under the year in which it was first copyrighted, but it does appear when its copyright was renewed in 1949, and in that entry its author, "Magus Incognito," is identified as one Clifford Edgar Brooksmith. In the National Union Catalogue of Pre-1956 Imprints both editions of the "Secret Doctrine of the Rosicrucians appear (1918 and 1949), and for each of them the anonymous author is identified as Clifford Edgar Brooksmith. So far as I can find out, this man never published any other book, and I have not yet managed to find out much else about him. There is just one "Clifford Brooksmith" listed in the Social Security Death Index. He was born in 1899 in Illinois, and died in 1979 in New York (city). If this is the right Clifford Brooksmith, he was only about 20 when he wrote his book.

The sum of all this: James North is mistaken, though it seems to be a sincere mistake, about Moriarty, Atkinson and Ramacharaka. I am inclined to explain Bowen's letter as reflecting some of Moriarty's own myth-building about his earlier achievements, as well as a certain amount of deliberate deception. Sampson Dunn may be meant as a "cover" for Moriarty himself.

comments.title: Age for abstract knowledge
comments.content:
I'll try to be succinct about this. I've encountered in a few places the idea that children shouldn't learn to read, (or other things that involve a lot of abstract thought), until they're seven years old. I believe that was Rudolf Steiner's perspective, hence Waldorf schools don't usually teach reading until after that age. Joseph Chilton Pearce says something similar in his book Magical Child.

As far as you know, is there any occult basis for this view? I imagine that Steiner didn't get it out of nowhere. From the little I know of his thought, he believed that the spiritual development of human beings took place in something like 7 year cycles. I'm especially curious what you think, given that the Magic Monday FAQ says that it's after 7 years old that children are safe around ritual magic. Is there any basis in the occult traditions you've encountered that suggests there would be risks or drawbacks to a pre-school child learning to read earlier than this? I forget if it was Steiner or someone else who warned that trying to get children to do and learn things in advance of when they're truly ready (even if they're interested and practically capable of doing so) could throw a wrench in the works of the development in terms of the soul or subtle bodies. Perhaps akin to the note in the FAQ that ids shouldn't learn ceremonial magic until after puberty.

Your thoughts? I'd also be interested in any sources that you can recommend which discuss childhood from the perspective of traditional occult philosophy, so that I can look up these questions myself.

replies.title: Re: Age for abstract knowledge
replies.content:
(Not JMG)

What did Steiner propose doing with kids like me, who figure it out on their own before age 7? Or was he not aware of any?

replies.title: Re: Age for abstract knowledge
replies.content: OP here: that is part of what I’m wondering!  I learned earlier too, and it’s clearly not the end of the world or anything. I’m partly wondering because I’ve read this guy named Glen Doman who has fascinating material about how even very young children can learn reading, math etc as early as babyhood - indeed in many cases learn much easier than many kids do around age 6 when they typically introduce it in schools - since babies and toddlers are sponges for everything they are exposed to. I find Doman’s stuff very compelling, and am interested in other perspectives. I’m all for tapping the maximum of human potential (that’s what Doman is all about), but am also wary of the notion of trying to turn kids into prodigies without a clear understanding of why and the larger implications.

replies.title: Re: Age for abstract knowledge
replies.content:
Yes, Steiner got his theory from a Theosophical teaching that has the various levels of the self maturing in seven year cycles. I'm far from sure what to think of it. I learned to read at two and a half, and it's quite possible that some of the consequences of that have not been good. Still, I don't think it's wise at all to impose one standard on all children, since every soul that comes into incarnation has different gifts, different karma, and a different life to live.

Steiner was one of the few people I know of in the occult tradition to get into things like educational theory; just off hand I can't think of anyone else who dealt with it in any detail.

replies.title: Re: Goals of Alchemy, Spread of Monotheism
replies.content:
1) Spagyrics has three main goals. The first is the production of natural medicines. The second is the creation of elixirs that will speed the spiritual evolution of the practitioner. The third is developing the necessary skills for the mineral and metallic work. Most spagyrists do all three.

2) Religions by and large are only good during one zodiacal age; when they survive into a second age, they do it by completely reformulating themselves. Think of the way that the temple worship of the ancient Israelites morphed into Rabbinic Judaism and you can get some sense of how this works! Most of the other faiths of the ancient world were not able to make that transition, and lost power as the Sun moved into Pisces at the equinox, while the Piscean faith of Christianity had plenty of power. Of course now the tables have turned...

comments.title: Adepthood
comments.content:
Good evening!

I've taken note lately of a couple of occultist authors who refer to themselves as Adepts. I'm wondering:

1. What does the title "adept" refer to? What do you mean when you use it?

2. Do you consider yourself an adept? If so, why not use the title?

3. What do you make of the practice of an occultist self-applying the label?

4. I've seen you refer to individuals as adepts (for instance Franz Bardon), but as I recall only dead individuals. Is their being dead significant for you in applying the label?

I promise I'm not trying to put you on the spot here, I'm just trying to get a better grasp of the term. Part of me is suspicious of people self-applying it, but then again, if someone's earned it, they have the right.

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Adepthood
replies.content:
I'm going to answer this in a slightly indirect way. In the Chinese martial arts there's a title, Sifu, which denotes a master-teacher of a martial arts lineage. You never call yourself Sifu; it's purely a title that other people use to refer to you, and they, not you, decide when you've earned it. Clueless white guys who go around labeling themselves "Sifu" are the subject of wry jokes among Chinese-American martial artists.

The title "saint" in Christianity is much the same, except it's usually not conferred on living people. You don't just up and call yourself a saint -- or if you do, everyone takes that as proof that you're not one. A person becomes recognized as a saint by others, and the various old Christian churches have more or less elaborate ways of formalizing the process.

The title "adept" falls somewhere in between those two examples. Technically speaking, it refers to an alchemist who has accomplished the Great Work, or (by extension) to a mage or occultist who has achieved a similar level of mastery in the work of self-transformation. A person who goes around proclaiming that he's an adept proves, by that proclamation, that he's not one. (To know, to dare, to will, and what?) There are living people I believe to be adepts, but as I haven't had the brass-balled nerve to grill them about their spiritual experiences I don't know that for certain. With some dead people, the question's pretty clear one way or the other, and so it's not at all inappropriate to refer to Bardon, say, as an adept; Dion Fortune described her teacher, Theodore Moriarty, as "an adept if there ever was one," but he was dead when she wrote that. It seems like a sensible habit to follow.
